posts.title: agents that work are boring
posts.hook: The AI agents that are actually working in production right now are boring as hell.
posts.body:
The AI agents that are actually working in production right now are boring as hell.

They're not reasoning through complex multi-step chains. They're not browsing the web autonomously. They're not replacing entire departments.

They're doing one thing well.

Parsing an email, enriching a record, routing a lead, drafting a first pass. Then a human checks it and hits send.

I run about 15 automated workflows across my GTM stack. The ones that survive past week two all share the same pattern:

✅ Single input, single output
✅ Human checkpoint before anything external happens
✅ Fallback to a default when the model gets confused
✅ Logging that actually tells you what went wrong

The ones I've killed? The ambitious ones. The "let the agent figure it out" ones. The ones where I gave it three tools and said go.

Every time I simplify an agent down to one job, reliability goes up and I stop babysitting it.

The gap between agent demos and agent production isn't intelligence. It's error handling.
posts.cta: What's your simplest agent that actually runs unsupervised?

remixes.label: unsexy-workhorse
remixes.hook: Nobody is posting about the AI agents that actually make money. Because they're embarrassingly simple.
remixes.body:
Nobody is posting about the AI agents that actually make money. Because they're embarrassingly simple.

The demos that go viral have agents browsing the web, writing code, orchestrating ten tools in sequence. Cool. None of that is running unattended at 3am making my business work.

What is running unattended:

A script that reads inbound emails, extracts company names, and enriches them against a CRM. Another that scores leads based on three signals and routes them to the right sequence. A third that drafts LinkedIn comments I review in batch every morning.

Each one does exactly one job. Each one fails in predictable ways. Each one has a human checkpoint before anything goes out the door.

I have about 15 of these running daily. Total development time per agent: maybe 2 hours. Total value: they replaced about 20 hours of weekly manual work.

The gap between what gets attention and what gets results in AI has never been wider. The agents that work are the ones nobody wants to demo. They parse, enrich, route, draft. Then stop.

If your agent needs a 10-minute video to explain what it does, it probably doesn't do it reliably yet.
remixes.cta: What's the most boring automation you run that you'd never want to do manually again?

remixes.label: demo-vs-production
remixes.hook: Every AI agent demo I see on Twitter does 47 things. Every AI agent I run in production does one.
remixes.body:
Every AI agent demo I see on Twitter does 47 things. Every AI agent I run in production does one.

There's a reason for this. Demos optimize for impressions. Production optimizes for not waking you up at 2am.

When I started building agents, I made the mistake of chaining everything together. Lead comes in, agent enriches it, scores it, writes the outreach, sends it, logs it. Beautiful in testing. Catastrophic in production.

One API timeout cascaded into a retry loop that sent 40 duplicate emails. That was the last time I built a complex agent.

Now every agent I run follows one rule: do one thing, then hand off. The enrichment agent enriches. The scoring agent scores. The drafting agent drafts. A human reviews the draft and clicks send.

The boring part is that this works. It's been working for months. I spend maybe 30 minutes a day reviewing outputs instead of 4 hours doing the work manually.

The exciting part is that nobody talks about this because single-task agents don't get engagement. But they get results.

Simplicity isn't a limitation. It's the entire architecture.
remixes.cta: Have you ever had a multi-step agent fail spectacularly in production? What did you simplify after?

The agents delivering real ROI in production are not the complex, multi-step orchestrators. They're single-purpose tools that do one job and do it well.

posts.title: limited compute forces better architecture
posts.hook: Working with limited compute taught me more about AI architecture than any tutorial.
posts.body:
Working with limited compute taught me more about AI architecture than any tutorial.

I run a local Qwen 2.5 14B on a Mac Mini. No A100s. No cloud GPU cluster. Just a desktop machine that also runs my dev server, my cron jobs, and my CRM sync.

Constraints I hit immediately:
- Can't fine-tune anything meaningful locally
- Inference is slow enough that batching matters
- Context windows are expensive so every token counts

What this forced me to build:

Tiered model routing. Local model handles high-frequency, low-stakes tasks like keyword matching and basic classification. API calls to Opus only when the task actually needs it.

Aggressive caching. If I enriched a company yesterday, I'm not enriching it again today.

Smaller prompts. When you're paying per token on one side and waiting per token on the other, you learn to say what you mean in fewer words.

Teams spending $50k/month on inference could probably cut that in half with better routing. Most API calls don't need your best model. They need your fastest one that's good enough.

Infrastructure constraints aren't limitations. They're architecture lessons you'd skip if you had unlimited budget.
posts.cta: Anyone else running tiered model routing? Curious what your split looks like between local and API.

remixes.label: constraint-as-teacher
remixes.hook: I can't afford to send every request to GPT-4. That constraint built a better system than any architecture doc.
remixes.body:
I can't afford to send every request to GPT-4. That constraint built a better system than any architecture doc.

When you have unlimited API budget, you get lazy. Every task goes to the biggest model. Every prompt is verbose. Every response gets processed once and thrown away.

When you're running a local 14B model on the same Mac Mini that hosts your dev server and cron jobs, you learn fast what actually needs intelligence and what just needs pattern matching.

My current setup uses three tiers:

Local Qwen 2.5 for high-frequency, low-stakes work. CRM enrichment, content tagging, log parsing. Runs hundreds of times daily, costs nothing.

Claude Sonnet for mid-tier work. Drafting, summarization, anything that needs decent language quality but not deep reasoning.

Claude Opus for the hard stuff. Architecture decisions, complex content, anything where quality directly impacts revenue.

90% of my daily AI calls hit the local model. Maybe 8% go to Sonnet. Opus handles the remaining 2%.

The result: my total API spend dropped by about 70% while output quality stayed the same. Because most tasks don't need the smartest model. They need the right model.

Constraints don't limit what you build. They sharpen how you build it.
remixes.cta: What's the cheapest model you're running that handles a surprisingly large share of your workload?

remixes.label: gpu-poor-advantage
remixes.hook: Not having a GPU cluster is the best thing that happened to my AI stack.
remixes.body:
Not having a GPU cluster is the best thing that happened to my AI stack.

I run everything off a single Mac Mini. Local inference with Qwen 2.5 14B. API calls to Claude when quality matters. No cloud GPUs. No training runs. No infrastructure team.

When I started, this felt like a limitation. Every tutorial assumed you had access to beefy compute. Every framework defaulted to the largest model available.

But working within these constraints forced decisions that turned out to be architecturally correct.

I had to classify which tasks actually need intelligence vs. which ones just need text processing. That classification became my routing layer.

I had to batch requests because inference was too slow for real-time. That batching pattern reduced my API costs by 60% when I applied it to cloud models too.

I had to cache aggressively because regenerating was expensive. That cache layer now serves most repeated queries without hitting any model at all.

Every optimization I built for the local model made the entire system better. Faster, cheaper, more reliable.

The teams I see struggling most with AI costs are the ones who started with unlimited compute and never had to think about routing. They're now trying to retrofit the discipline that constraints teach you on day one.
remixes.cta: Have resource constraints ever forced you into a better architecture than you would have designed on purpose?

posts.title: agentic coding changed my build speed
posts.hook: I shipped more working code in March than I did in all of Q4. The difference wasn't skill. It was tooling.
posts.body:
I shipped more working code in March than I did in all of Q4. The difference wasn't skill. It was tooling.

I've been building with Claude Code as my primary dev environment for about two months now. Not as autocomplete. As a collaborator that reads my codebase, understands my architecture, and writes implementation code.

What changed practically:

🔧 Context handoffs between sessions mean I don't lose state. Close the laptop, come back the next morning, pick up exactly where I left off.

🔧 Subagents handle the boring stuff in parallel. Research, file exploration, test running. I focus on decisions.

🔧 Build verification is automated. Every change gets checked before I see it.

What didn't change: I still make all the architectural decisions. I still review every diff. I still debug the weird edge cases.

The tool didn't replace my judgment. It removed the friction between having an idea and seeing it work. The bottleneck moved from typing to thinking, which is where it should've been the whole time.

Solo developers building with agentic coding tools aren't competing with other solo developers anymore. They're competing with small teams. That's the actual shift happening right now.
posts.cta: If you're building with agentic coding tools, what's the one workflow that changed things most for you?

remixes.label: before-after-workflow
remixes.hook: Two months ago I spent 45 minutes context-switching back into a codebase after lunch. Now I spend zero.
remixes.body:
Two months ago I spent 45 minutes context-switching back into a codebase after lunch. Now I spend zero.

The shift wasn't discipline or better note-taking. It was building with an AI coding tool that maintains context across sessions.

Here's what my workflow looks like now with Claude Code:

At the end of every session, a context handoff document gets generated automatically. It captures what I was building, what's done, what's blocked, and what comes next.

Next session, the tool reads that handoff, checks git status, and picks up exactly where I left off. No re-reading code. No trying to remember what I was thinking.

The other shift: I don't write boilerplate anymore. Routes, API handlers, database queries, component scaffolding. I describe the behavior I want and review what gets generated. My job moved from typing code to reviewing code.

That review step is important. I'm not blindly accepting output. I'm reading every line, testing edge cases, catching architectural mistakes. The AI handles volume. I handle judgment.

In March I shipped a full CRM sync pipeline, a multi-site blog system, and a lead scoring engine. All on a solo team. Previously that would have been a quarter of work with two developers.

The speed gain isn't about writing faster. It's about eliminating the dead time between thinking and having working code.
remixes.cta: What's the biggest time sink in your dev workflow that tooling hasn't solved yet?

remixes.label: collaborator-not-copilot
remixes.hook: I stopped treating AI coding tools as autocomplete. That's when my output tripled.
remixes.body:
I stopped treating AI coding tools as autocomplete. That's when my output tripled.

Most developers I talk to use AI for code completion. Tab to accept a suggestion, move on. It's useful, maybe a 20% speed boost on typing.

But that's not where the real leverage is.

I use Claude Code as a collaborator that reads my entire codebase, understands the architecture, and writes full implementations. Not snippets. Functions, routes, tests, migrations.

The workflow: I describe what I need in plain language, including constraints and edge cases. The tool reads the relevant files, proposes an implementation, and I review it. If something's off, I give feedback and it iterates.

This changes what's possible as a solo developer. In March I shipped more production code than I did in all of Q4. Not because I typed faster. Because the bottleneck moved from implementation to decision-making.

Three specific things that changed:

Context handoffs between sessions eliminated re-onboarding time. I pick up exactly where I left off.

Skill files that teach the AI my project conventions mean I don't repeat the same instructions.

Plan mode forces me to think through architecture before writing code, which cuts rework by half.

The tool handles volume. I handle taste and judgment. That split is where the leverage lives.
remixes.cta: Are you using AI coding tools as autocomplete or as a full collaborator? Curious where you've found the most leverage.

posts.title: agent failures are durability failures
posts.hook: Most agent failures in production aren't intelligence failures. They're durability failures.
posts.body:
Most agent failures in production aren't intelligence failures. They're durability failures.

The agent figured out the right answer. Then the function timed out. Or the connection dropped. Or the process crashed mid-chain and nobody knew where to resume.

I've been running automated GTM workflows on serverless infrastructure for months. The pattern that keeps showing up:

Step 1 works. Step 2 works. Step 3 calls an external API that takes 8 seconds. Step 4 never runs because the function hit its timeout.

The fix isn't making the model faster or smarter. It's making the execution durable.

➡️ Break long chains into discrete steps with checkpoints
➡️ Store intermediate state so you can resume from where you crashed
➡️ Set up dead letter queues for the steps that fail silently
➡️ Log every step transition, not just the final output

For agent workloads, you usually need the opposite of fast. You need patient. You need "I'll wait 30 seconds for that API and pick up where I left off if something breaks."

The infrastructure layer for agents matters more than the model layer. A mediocre model on durable infrastructure beats a great model on fragile infrastructure every time.
posts.cta: What's your approach to handling mid-chain failures in automated workflows?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Talks about building AI-first infrastructure for edge deployment of agentic apps, focusing on serverless automation and real-time inference. Details Vercel's updates for AI workflows and low-latency tools. Stresses integration with existing dev stacks.

remixes.label: crash-recovery-pattern
remixes.hook: My agent got the right answer, then the server crashed, and nobody knew where to pick up. That failure taught me more than any success.
remixes.body:
My agent got the right answer, then the server crashed, and nobody knew where to pick up. That failure taught me more than any success.

I was running a GTM workflow. Four steps: detect signal, enrich lead, score fit, draft outreach. Steps 1 through 3 completed. Step 4 timed out. The function restarted from scratch. Re-enriched the same lead. Re-scored it. Then failed on step 4 again.

Three retries later I had four duplicate enrichment records and zero outreach sent.

The problem wasn't the AI. The model nailed every step. The problem was that my execution layer had no memory. It couldn't answer the question: where did I leave off?

This is the pattern I keep seeing in agent failures. The intelligence works. The plumbing doesn't.

What fixed it: breaking the workflow into individually durable steps. Each step writes its result before the next one starts. If the process dies, it checks what's already done and resumes from the last completed step.

It's not glamorous. It's basically the same pattern that databases solved decades ago with write-ahead logs. But applied to AI workflows, it's the difference between an agent that works in demos and one that works at 3am.

Reliability isn't an AI problem. It's an infrastructure problem wearing an AI costume.
remixes.cta: Have you hit the wall where your agent works perfectly in testing but falls apart in production? What broke first?

remixes.label: serverless-trap
remixes.hook: Serverless is great for AI agents until step 3 of 5 times out and your whole workflow evaporates.
remixes.body:
Serverless is great for AI agents until step 3 of 5 times out and your whole workflow evaporates.

I've been running automated GTM workflows on serverless infrastructure for months. The failure mode is always the same: individual steps work fine, but the chain is fragile.

Function A calls an enrichment API. Takes 2 seconds. Works every time.

Function B scores the lead. Takes 500ms. Bulletproof.

Function C calls an LLM to draft outreach. Takes 8 seconds on a good day. 45 seconds when the provider is slow. Timeout is 30 seconds.

So Function C fails intermittently. And when it fails, the entire workflow restarts because there's no checkpoint between steps.

The fix isn't longer timeouts. It's durable execution.

Each step persists its output independently. The orchestrator tracks which steps completed. On failure, it resumes from the last checkpoint instead of restarting from zero.

This pattern, step-based execution with crash recovery, is old. Databases have done it forever. Job queues have done it for decades. But in the rush to build AI agents, most teams skip it.

They get the intelligence right and the durability wrong. Then they're surprised when their demo agent that worked perfectly in testing fails unpredictably in production.

The agent isn't broken. The execution model is.
remixes.cta: What's the longest multi-step AI workflow you've gotten to run reliably in production?

tones.builder:
Most agent failures in production aren't intelligence failures. They're durability failures.

The agent figured out the right answer. Then the function timed out. Or the connection dropped. Or the process crashed mid-chain and nobody knew where to resume.

I've been running automated GTM workflows on serverless infrastructure for months. The pattern that keeps showing up:

Step 1 works. Step 2 works. Step 3 calls an external API that takes 8 seconds. Step 4 never runs because the function hit its timeout.

The fix isn't making the model faster or smarter. It's making the execution durable.

➡️ Break long chains into discrete steps with checkpoints
➡️ Store intermediate state so you can resume from where you crashed
➡️ Set up dead letter queues for the steps that fail silently
➡️ Log every step transition, not just the final output

For agent workloads, you usually need the opposite of fast. You need patient. You need "I'll wait 30 seconds for that API and pick up where I left off if something breaks."

The infrastructure layer for agents matters more than the model layer. A mediocre model on durable infrastructure beats a great model on fragile infrastructure every time.
tones.advisor:
If you're running multi-step AI agents in production, I'd prioritize durability over intelligence.

The pattern I see repeatedly: the model gets the right answer, but the infrastructure can't sustain the execution. A function times out. A connection drops. The process crashes mid-chain and there's no way to resume.

Here's what I recommend for any workflow longer than two steps.

Break the chain into individually durable steps. Each step writes its result to persistent storage before triggering the next one. If the process dies at step 3, it checks steps 1 and 2 on restart and picks up where it left off.

Add idempotency to every step. If a step runs twice with the same input, it should produce the same output without side effects. This prevents the duplicate records and double-sends that plague naive retry logic.

Set up dead letter handling. When a step fails after max retries, capture the full state and route it for manual review instead of silently dropping it.

This is essentially write-ahead logging applied to AI workflows. It's not new or clever. But teams skip it because they're focused on the intelligence layer, and then spend weeks debugging production failures that have nothing to do with the AI.
tones.provocateur:
Your AI agent isn't failing because it's not smart enough. It's failing because your infrastructure is from 2019.

I keep watching teams pour effort into prompt engineering, fine-tuning, and model selection while running their agents on fire-and-forget serverless functions with no checkpointing, no resume capability, and retry logic that restarts from scratch.

The agent figured out the right answer. Then the function timed out. Then it ran again and enriched the same lead twice. Then it timed out again. Three duplicate records, zero results.

This isn't an AI problem. It's a systems engineering problem that the AI hype wave convinced everyone to ignore.

Durable execution. Step-based checkpointing. Idempotent operations. Write-ahead logs. These patterns are decades old. They work. And almost nobody building agents is using them.

: most agent failures in production would be fixed by a junior backend engineer who's never touched a prompt. Because the failure isn't in the reasoning. It's in the plumbing.

Stop optimizing the brain. Fix the body.

posts.title: gtm engineering is just engineering for revenue
posts.hook: The best GTM teams I'm seeing right now don't have a VP of Sales and a VP of Marketing arguing about attribution. They have an engineer who built the pipeline.
posts.body:
The best GTM teams I'm seeing right now don't have a VP of Sales and a VP of Marketing arguing about attribution. They have an engineer who built the pipeline.

GTM engineering isn't a buzzword. It's what happens when you treat revenue generation like a system instead of a series of disconnected departments.

What that looks like in practice:

🕷️ Automated signal detection. Job postings, tech stack changes, funding rounds. Scraped, scored, and routed before a human touches it.

🔧 Enrichment chains. Company data flows through Clay, gets cross-referenced against ICP scoring, and lands in your CRM with context attached. Not just a name and email.

✅ Outbound triggered by behavior, not by calendar. "Send 200 emails on Tuesday" becomes "send a message when this account does something that indicates buying intent."

The SDR who manually built buying committees five years ago had the right instinct. They were tracking signals in spreadsheets, personalizing every message, building context by hand.

Now we can automate the pattern they discovered. The judgment stays human. The execution becomes systematic.

Teams that figure this out are running circles around teams still arguing about MQL definitions.
posts.cta: What signal are you using to trigger outbound that's actually working right now?

remixes.label: systems-over-departments
remixes.hook: I replaced a 3-person outbound team with a Python script, a CRM, and better signal detection. Not because AI is magic. Because the old process was just bad.
remixes.body:
I replaced a 3-person outbound team with a Python script, a CRM, and better signal detection. Not because AI is magic. Because the old process was just bad.

The traditional GTM motion: marketing generates leads based on demographics. Sales qualifies them manually. Half the pipeline is dead on arrival because nobody checked if the company actually needs what you sell.

The engineered GTM motion: automated signals detect intent. A script monitors job postings, tech stack changes, funding rounds, and content engagement. When multiple signals converge on one account, it gets enriched, scored, and routed to a sequence.

No human touches it until the outreach draft is ready for review.

This isn't theoretical. I run this pipeline daily. It monitors about 200 target accounts across three signal types. When signals fire, leads get enriched through Apollo, scored in my CRM, and routed to personalized sequences.

The output: outbound that actually converts because every message goes to someone showing real buying signals, not just someone who fits a demographic profile.

GTM engineering isn't about replacing salespeople. It's about giving them a pipeline that's already warm by the time they touch it. The engineer doesn't replace the team. The engineer makes the team's time worth more.
remixes.cta: What signal has the highest conversion rate in your outbound motion right now?

remixes.label: death-of-attribution-fights
remixes.hook: Marketing says the lead came from the webinar. Sales says it came from the cold email. Neither is right. The signal detection script found them two weeks before either team knew they existed.
remixes.body:
Marketing says the lead came from the webinar. Sales says it came from the cold email. Neither is right. The signal detection script found them two weeks before either team knew they existed.

Attribution arguments exist because of gaps in the system. When you can't trace how a lead actually entered your pipeline, departments fight over credit. When the system is instrumented end to end, the data speaks for itself.

GTM engineering eliminates attribution fights by making the pipeline transparent.

Here's what that looks like in practice:

A signal detection script monitors target accounts for intent signals. Job postings with relevant keywords. Tech stack changes. Funding announcements. Content engagement on specific topics.

When signals converge, the account gets flagged automatically. Enrichment data gets pulled. A fit score gets calculated. If the score passes a threshold, outreach gets drafted and queued for review.

Every step is logged. You can trace any deal back to the exact signal that triggered it, the enrichment data that qualified it, and the sequence that converted it.

No more arguing about which department gets credit. The system shows the whole path.

This is what happens when you treat revenue generation as an engineering problem instead of a turf war. The pipeline becomes visible, and the arguments become irrelevant.
remixes.cta: How much time does your team spend on attribution debates vs. actually improving the pipeline?

If you're building or rebuilding your GTM motion, I'd recommend thinking about it as an engineering problem first.

The teams getting the best results right now have collapsed the wall between marketing and sales by building a unified pipeline with automated signal detection, enrichment, scoring, and routing.

Here's what I recommend as a starting architecture:

Automated signal detection. Monitor target accounts for job postings, tech stack changes, funding events, and content engagement. Multiple converging signals indicate real intent.

Programmatic enrichment. When signals fire, pull company and contact data automatically through tools like Apollo or similar.

Scoring and routing. Calculate fit scores based on signals plus firmographic data. Route qualified leads to personalized sequences based on their specific signals.

Human review at the outreach stage. The system does the research and drafts the message. A human reviews tone, accuracy, and timing before anything goes out.

This approach works because it front-loads the qualification work that sales teams traditionally do manually. By the time a rep sees a lead, it's already warm and contextualized.

The investment is engineering time upfront. The payoff is a pipeline where every outbound touchpoint is backed by real buying signals instead of demographic guesses.

Your VP of Sales and VP of Marketing are arguing about attribution while a solo founder with a Python script is eating their pipeline.

The traditional GTM org chart is broken. Marketing generates leads based on job titles and company sizes. Sales cold calls from a list. Both teams measure different KPIs and blame each other when revenue misses.

Meanwhile, the engineering-first GTM motion just works.

Automated signal detection watches for actual buying intent. Not "downloaded a whitepaper" intent. Real signals. Hiring for roles that use your product. Switching off a competitor's tech stack. Raising a round that funds the problem you solve.

When signals converge, the lead gets enriched, scored, and routed to a personalized sequence. A human reviews the draft and sends it. Total manual time per qualified lead: maybe 3 minutes.

You don't need a 30-person GTM team. You need one engineer who understands that revenue generation is a systems problem. The pipeline is code. The qualification logic is code. The routing is code.

The companies that figure this out first will outperform teams ten times their size. The companies that don't will keep having attribution meetings.

posts.title: personalization at scale is a data problem
posts.hook: "Personalized outbound at scale" is a phrase everyone says and almost nobody does well. The gap isn't the sending tool. It's the data layer underneath it.
posts.body:
"Personalized outbound at scale" is a phrase everyone says and almost nobody does well. The gap isn't the sending tool. It's the data layer underneath it.

I've tested this across hundreds of accounts.

Bad personalization: "Hey {{first_name}}, I noticed you're the {{title}} at {{company}}. Companies like yours often struggle with..."

That's mail merge with extra steps. The recipient knows it.

Good personalization: referencing something specific about their stack, their recent hires, their content, or a shift in their market that connects to your offer.

But good personalization requires good data. And good data requires enrichment that goes deeper than Clearbit basics.

The workflow I've landed on:

1. Pull the account from your ICP list
2. Run enrichment across 3-4 sources (not just one)
3. Score which data points are actually relevant to your message
4. Template around the scored signals, not around static fields
5. Human review before send. Always.

Step 3 is where most teams skip. They have the data but don't filter it. So you get emails that reference a company's founding year because the model found it, not because it matters.

Personalization isn't about how much you know. It's about knowing which thing to mention.
posts.cta: What data point consistently gets the best reply rates in your outbound?

remixes.label: reverse-engineering-replies
remixes.hook: I tracked reply rates across 400+ cold outbound sequences last quarter. The sequences that got responses had one thing in common. And it wasn't the copy.
remixes.body:
I tracked reply rates across 400+ cold outbound sequences last quarter. The sequences that got responses had one thing in common. And it wasn't the copy.

It was the research layer before the copy ever got written.

The top-performing sequences referenced something the recipient actually did. Not their job title. Not their company size. Something specific. A podcast appearance. A product launch. A hiring pattern that signaled a strategic shift.

This is not magic. It's a data pipeline.

Step one: enrich beyond the basics. Job title and company name are table stakes. You need trigger events, tech stack changes, funding rounds, content they've published.

Step two: build snippet logic that turns raw data into a single relevant sentence. Not a paragraph. One line that proves you did the work.

Step three: test relentlessly. The data point that works for series A founders is completely different from what works for enterprise VPs.

Most teams skip step one entirely. They buy a list, plug it into a sequence tool, and wonder why reply rates sit at 2%.

The outbound teams winning right now aren't better writers. They're better researchers. The writing is easy once you actually know something worth saying.
remixes.cta: What's one enrichment data point that consistently moves the needle for your team?

remixes.label: death-of-merge-tags
remixes.hook: Merge tags killed cold email. Not spam filters. Not inbox fatigue. The merge tag itself.
remixes.body:
Merge tags killed cold email. Not spam filters. Not inbox fatigue. The merge tag itself.

When everyone got access to {{first_name}} and {{company}}, personalization became indistinguishable from automation. Buyers learned to pattern-match. They can spot a sequence email in under three seconds.

So what actually works?

I've been running a different approach. Instead of personalizing the template, I personalize the research. The template stays simple. The insight changes per account.

Here's what that looks like in practice. I pull three signals before writing: what the company shipped recently, what roles they're hiring for, and what their leadership is talking about publicly. Then I write one sentence that connects those dots to a real problem.

No "I noticed you're the VP of..."
No "Companies like yours often..."
No "I'd love to pick your brain..."

Just a specific observation that proves I understand their world. That's it. One sentence of genuine insight wrapped in a short email.

The reply rate difference is absurd. Generic sequences hover around 1-3%. Signal-based sequences consistently hit 12-18% in my tests.

The tool doesn't matter. Lemlist, Instantly, Apollo. They all send emails. The differentiation is upstream. It's what you know before you write.
remixes.cta: Have you measured the difference between template personalization and research-based personalization?

tones.advisor:
If you're running outbound at scale and reply rates are below 5%, the problem is almost certainly your data layer, not your copy.

Here's what I recommend looking at first. Pull your last 50 positive replies and categorize what personalization element actually triggered the response. You'll find it's rarely the prospect's name or title. It's usually a specific reference to something they did, built, or decided.

The fix is structural. Build an enrichment pipeline that captures trigger events, not just firmographic data. Job changes, product launches, funding rounds, hiring surges, public content. These are the signals that make outbound feel like a conversation instead of a broadcast.

Once you have those signals, your templates get simpler, not more complex. One line of genuine insight outperforms three paragraphs of flattery every time.

The teams I see winning at outbound right now have invested in their data infrastructure the way most teams invest in their sequence tools. They spend 70% of their effort on research and enrichment, 30% on writing and sending.

If you flip that ratio, you'll see reply rates move within two weeks. Start with your enrichment layer. Everything downstream improves when the inputs are better.
tones.provocateur:
Your outbound is failing and you keep blaming the copy. Stop it.

The copy is fine. Your data is garbage. That's the actual problem nobody wants to talk about because fixing data infrastructure is boring and buying a new sequence tool feels productive.

Here's . Every "personalized" email that starts with "Hey {{first_name}}, I noticed you're the {{title}} at {{company}}" gets deleted before the second sentence. The recipient knows you ran a CSV through a mail merge. You're not fooling anyone.

Real personalization requires real research. And real research requires a data pipeline that most teams are too lazy to build.

I've tested this extensively. When you reference something a prospect actually did, said, or shipped, reply rates jump from 2% to 15%. That's not a marginal improvement. That's a fundamentally different channel.

But here's what bothers me. The industry keeps selling personalization as a feature of the sending tool. "AI-powered personalization" that's just rewriting the same template five ways. It's still fake. The prospect still knows.

The competitive advantage isn't in how you send. It's in what you know before you send. And most of you aren't even trying to solve that problem.

posts.title: solo builders have an unfair advantage now
posts.hook: Solo developers shipping products in 2026 have an unfair advantage that didn't exist two years ago. And it's not what most people think.
posts.body:
Solo developers shipping products in 2026 have an unfair advantage that didn't exist two years ago. And it's not what most people think.

It's not AI writing your code for you. It's AI handling the parts of building a business that used to require hiring.

Content generation. Lead enrichment. Customer research. Data pipeline maintenance. Email sequences. SEO analysis.

I run a one-person GTM engineering practice. My "team" is a set of automated workflows, a local LLM for high-frequency tasks, API calls to Claude for complex work, and cron jobs that run every night while I sleep.

Yesterday's automated output:
- Daily blog post generated and published
- SEO keyword tracking updated
- Reddit and LinkedIn scouted for engagement opportunities
- CRM records enriched with new signals
- Portfolio signals checked

None of that required me to be awake.

The solo builder advantage isn't about doing everything yourself. It's about automating the repetitive parts so your human hours go toward things that actually need judgment. Product decisions. Relationship building. Creative work.

Two years ago this stack would've cost $15k/month in SaaS tools and a part-time VA. Now it costs API tokens and the time to build the pipelines once.
posts.cta: What's the one workflow you automated that gave you back the most hours?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey of shipping multiple products as a solo developer, including launching a new AI tool in under a week and achieving $10k MRR quickly. He emphasizes the power of building in public and iterating based on real user feedback. Key takeaway: Focus on velocity over perfection to validate ideas fast.

remixes.label: hidden-leverage-points
remixes.hook: The most overpowered thing about being a solo builder in 2026 isn't AI code generation. It's that you can now automate the business side too.
remixes.body:
The most overpowered thing about being a solo builder in 2026 isn't AI code generation. It's that you can now automate the business side too.

Two years ago, if you were a solo developer, you could build the product. But everything around the product, marketing, lead gen, customer research, content, SEO, required either hiring or doing it all manually.

That calculus changed.

I run a full GTM engine by myself. Not because I'm working 80 hour weeks. Because most of the repetitive business operations are automated.

My content pipeline generates drafts, runs SEO analysis, and schedules distribution. My lead enrichment pulls company signals and scores accounts without me touching a spreadsheet. My email sequences trigger based on behavioral data, not calendar reminders.

None of this is theoretical. I shipped all of it. The total monthly cost is less than one contractor's weekly rate.

The builders who are struggling right now are the ones still treating AI as a code assistant. That's the smallest part of the advantage. The real leverage is pointing automation at every business function that used to require a person.

You don't need a marketing team. You need a marketing pipeline. You don't need a sales team. You need a data-enriched outbound system.

Solo doesn't mean alone anymore. It means automated.
remixes.cta: What business function outside of coding have you successfully automated?

remixes.label: team-of-one-playbook
remixes.hook: I replaced five roles with automation this year. Here's the honest breakdown of what worked, what failed, and what still needs a human.
remixes.body:
I replaced five roles with automation this year. Here's the honest breakdown of what worked, what failed, and what still needs a human.

Content writer: 80% automated. AI drafts from structured prompts, I edit for voice and substance. Time savings: roughly 15 hours per week.

SEO analyst: 90% automated. Daily keyword tracking, competitor monitoring, content gap analysis. All runs on cron jobs. I review a dashboard once a day.

Lead researcher: 85% automated. Company enrichment, signal detection, account scoring. I review the shortlist and make final calls on who to reach out to.

Email ops: 70% automated. Sequences trigger on signals. But reply handling still needs a human. Nuance matters in conversations.

Design: 30% automated at best. AI-generated visuals work for quick social graphics. Anything customer-facing still needs real design work.

The pattern is clear. Structured, repetitive, data-driven work automates well. Anything requiring taste, judgment, or relationship building doesn't.

The mistake I see other solo builders make is trying to automate everything or automating nothing. The advantage is knowing which 70% to hand off and which 30% to protect.

Total cost of my automation stack: under $200 per month. Total time recovered: roughly 30 hours per week. That's the math that makes solo viable.
remixes.cta: Which role would you automate first if you could only pick one?

The advantage solo developers have in 2026 isn't writing code faster with AI. That's useful but incremental. The structural shift is that every business function surrounding the product can now be partially or fully automated at low cost.

I'd recommend mapping your current business operations across five categories: content, lead generation, customer research, data pipelines, and outbound communication. For each one, identify what's repetitive, data-driven, and doesn't require human judgment.

posts.title: pivot infrastructure matters more than courage
posts.hook: The narrative around career pivots is all wrong. It's not about having the courage to leap. It's about having the infrastructure to land.
posts.body:
The narrative around career pivots is all wrong. It's not about having the courage to leap. It's about having the infrastructure to land.

I'm watching a pattern repeat across my network. Engineers leave big tech. Sales leaders leave enterprise. Marketers leave agencies. They all want to build their own thing.

The ones who survive the first six months share three traits:

1. They built distribution before they built product. A newsletter, a LinkedIn presence, a community. Something that gives them an audience on day one, not day ninety.

2. They picked a wedge, not a platform. One specific problem for one specific buyer. Not "we help companies grow" but "we automate enrichment for outbound teams running Clay."

3. They shipped before they were ready. The first version was ugly. It worked. That's all that mattered.

The ones who struggle? They spent three months on a logo, a website, and a pitch deck. Then realized they had no pipeline and no proof of concept.

Building in public isn't a marketing strategy. It's a survival strategy. Every post is a signal. Every build log is a case study. Every workflow you share is proof that you know what you're doing.

The pivot itself is the easy part. The infrastructure around it determines whether it sticks.
posts.cta: If you've made the jump, what did you wish you'd built before leaving?

inspired_by.author: Arvid Kahl
inspired_by.summary: Arvid recounts pivoting from corporate job to bootstrapping his first SaaS product solo, detailing the challenges of learning tech stacks on the fly and shipping MVP in 3 months. He advises on audience-first building and shares revenue milestones achieved through public updates. Highlights the importance of niche communities for early traction.

remixes.label: infrastructure-audit
remixes.hook: Before you quit your job to build something, answer these five questions. If you can't answer at least three, you're not ready. And that's fine.
remixes.body:
Before you quit your job to build something, answer these five questions. If you can't answer at least three, you're not ready. And that's fine.

1. Do you have a distribution channel that doesn't depend on your employer's brand? A newsletter, a following, a community presence. Something that's yours.

2. Do you have six months of runway without touching savings you'd panic about losing? Real runway. Not theoretical "I could cut back" runway.

3. Do you have at least one revenue-generating skill you can sell within 30 days of leaving? Not a product. A skill. Consulting, freelancing, advisory work. Something that pays while you build.

4. Do you have a system for generating leads that doesn't require you to manually hustle every day? Even a basic one. A website that ranks. A content pipeline. An email list.

5. Have you shipped something outside of work in the last 90 days? Not planned. Not designed. Shipped. Functioning. Used by someone other than you.

Three out of five means you've built infrastructure. You have something to land on.

Two or fewer means you're relying on courage alone. And courage burns out in about 90 days when the bank account gets uncomfortable.

The pivot narrative glorifies the leap. Nobody talks about the landing gear.
remixes.cta: How many of these five did you have when you made your jump?

remixes.label: survival-pattern-analysis
remixes.hook: I've watched 20+ people leave corporate jobs to go independent in the last year. The survival rate is ugly. But the survivors share a pattern.
remixes.body:
I've watched 20+ people leave corporate jobs to go independent in the last year. The survival rate is ugly. But the survivors share a pattern.

The ones who crashed out within six months had one thing in common. They optimized for escape velocity. Get out fast. Figure it out later. Trust the process.

The ones who are still standing built three things before they left.

First, distribution. They had an audience, however small. A newsletter with 500 subscribers. A LinkedIn presence that got consistent engagement. A community where people knew their name.

Second, a sellable skill on day one. Not the product they wanted to build. A consulting offer, a freelance service, something that generates revenue immediately while the bigger thing takes shape.

Third, systems, not just ideas. Automated pipelines for content, for leads, for staying visible. They didn't depend on daily motivation to keep the business running.

The pattern is clear. Infrastructure compounds. Courage depletes.

I'm not against taking risks. I've taken plenty. But the framing of "just leap" is actively harmful. It conflates recklessness with bravery.

The smart pivots aren't brave. They're boring. Months of quiet building before the public move. That's what survival looks like.
remixes.cta: What infrastructure do you wish you'd built before going independent?

If you're considering a career pivot, here's the framework I'd recommend before making any public moves.

The common advice is to follow your passion and take the leap. That advice gets people hurt. The founders and independents who consistently survive the first year approach it differently.

I'd suggest building across three dimensions before leaving your current role.

First, distribution. Build an audience channel you own. A newsletter, a content presence, a community reputation. This takes 3-6 months of consistent effort. Start now, while you still have income stability.

Second, a day-one revenue skill. Not your dream product. A consulting or freelance offer based on expertise you already have. This funds the runway while you build the real thing.

Third, operational systems. Automate your content pipeline, your lead generation, and your visibility engine. When you're independent, motivation fluctuates. Systems don't.

The sequence matters too. Distribution first, because it's the slowest to build. Revenue skill second, because it gives you confidence. Systems third, because they multiply everything else.

I've watched dozens of people make this transition. The ones with infrastructure survive. The ones running on courage alone typically don't make it past month four. Build your landing gear before you jump.

The "just take the leap" crowd is giving dangerous advice. And they're usually giving it from a position of privilege they don't acknowledge.

Easy to say "follow your passion" when you've got a working spouse, family money, or a network that hands you consulting gigs. Most people leaving corporate don't have that safety net.

Here's about career pivots. Courage is the least important ingredient. It's basically free. Everyone has courage for about 90 days. Then the bank account starts talking and courage shuts up real fast.

What actually determines survival? Infrastructure.

Do you have an audience that knows your name? Do you have a skill you can sell in the first 30 days? Do you have systems that keep your business visible even on the days you can't get out of bed?

Most people who "leap" have none of these. They have a vision board, some savings, and a LinkedIn post announcing their departure. That's not a strategy. That's a countdown clock.

I'm not being cynical. I've made the jump myself. But I built the landing gear first. And watching people skip that step and crater hurts, because the advice ecosystem told them infrastructure doesn't matter. It does. It's the only thing that does.

posts.title: content strategy without CRM data is guessing
posts.hook: Most B2B content strategies are built on keyword research and vibes. The best ones are built on CRM data.
posts.body:
Most B2B content strategies are built on keyword research and vibes. The best ones are built on CRM data.

If you look at your closed-won deals from the last quarter, you'll find patterns in how those buyers found you, what content they engaged with, and what questions they asked during the sales cycle.

That's your content strategy. Not what SEMrush says is trending. What your actual buyers care about.

The workflow:

1. Pull closed-won deals from your CRM
2. Tag the content touchpoints in the buyer journey. What did they read, click, or reference on calls?
3. Cluster the topics. You'll usually find 3-5 themes
4. Build content around those clusters, not around industry keywords
5. Route that content into the channels where your ICP actually spends time

I started doing this with my own pipeline. The posts that drive actual conversations aren't the ones optimized for impressions. They're the ones that address a specific pain point I heard on a call three weeks ago.

Content that converts doesn't come from content strategy templates. It comes from listening to what your buyers actually say and writing about that.

The SEO play and the sales play should be the same play. If they're not, one of them is wrong.
posts.cta: How are you connecting your content strategy back to actual deal data?

inspired_by.author: Aja Frost
inspired_by.summary: Outlines a content strategy playbook for B2B SaaS, focusing on aligning content with sales funnels and outbound efforts. Highlights the role of SEO and personalized content in boosting conversion rates. Provides examples from HubSpot's recent campaigns.

remixes.label: crm-content-loop
remixes.hook: Your best content ideas are buried in your CRM. Not in keyword tools. Not in competitor analysis. In the actual words your buyers used before they signed.
remixes.body:
Your best content ideas are buried in your CRM. Not in keyword tools. Not in competitor analysis. In the actual words your buyers used before they signed.

I started pulling call transcripts and email threads from our last 30 closed-won deals and categorizing the questions buyers asked during the sales cycle. Three patterns jumped out immediately.

First, the questions they asked in discovery calls were almost never represented in our blog content. We were writing for keywords. They were searching for answers to problems we weren't addressing.

Second, the objections they raised mapped directly to content gaps. Every "but what about..." in a sales call is a blog post, a case study, or an FAQ that should already exist.

Third, the language they used was different from the language we used. We called it "workflow automation." They called it "getting my team to stop doing the same thing twice." That phrasing difference matters enormously for SEO and ad copy.

The fix is straightforward. Once a month, pull your closed-won data. Extract the questions, objections, and exact phrases buyers used. Build your content calendar around those, not around what a keyword tool suggests.

This isn't revolutionary. It's just connecting two systems that most companies keep completely separate. CRM data should feed content strategy. In most orgs, it doesn't.
remixes.cta: When was the last time your content team looked at closed-won deal data?

remixes.label: keyword-research-is-broken
remixes.hook: Keyword research tools show you what everyone is searching for. CRM data shows you what your actual buyers care about. These are not the same thing.
remixes.body:
Keyword research tools show you what everyone is searching for. CRM data shows you what your actual buyers care about. These are not the same thing.

I ran an experiment last quarter. Created two content tracks running in parallel.

Track one: traditional keyword research. High volume terms, competitive analysis, SEO best practices. Standard playbook.

Track two: CRM-sourced topics. Pulled directly from sales call transcripts, deal notes, and customer support tickets. Zero keyword research involved.

The results after 90 days were clear.

Track one generated more traffic. Significantly more. The keyword-researched content ranked well and brought visitors.

Track two generated more pipeline. The CRM-sourced content attracted fewer visitors, but those visitors were dramatically more likely to book a demo, reply to outbound, or convert on a form.

The lesson: volume and relevance are different metrics. And most content strategies optimize for volume because it's easier to measure and easier to report on.

But if your content team is measured on pipeline contribution, not page views, the strategy should start with deal data. What did buyers ask? What objections did they raise? What language did they use to describe their problems?

That's your editorial calendar. Everything else is noise that might drive traffic but won't drive revenue.
remixes.cta: Are you measuring content success by traffic or by pipeline contribution?

posts.title: browser AI is the next data advantage
posts.hook: Your browser is about to become your most powerful automation tool. Not your IDE. Not your terminal. Your browser.
posts.body:
Your browser is about to become your most powerful automation tool. Not your IDE. Not your terminal. Your browser.

I've been running Playwright-based automation for months. Scraping, form filling, data extraction. Standard stuff. But the shift happening now is different.

Browser-native AI isn't just automating clicks. It's understanding context. Reading a page, deciding what matters, extracting structured data without you writing a single CSS selector.

Practical example from this week: I needed to pull pricing data from 30 competitor pages. Old way, I'd write 30 different scrapers because every site structures pricing differently. New way, I pointed an LLM at each page and said "extract the pricing tiers." Same prompt. Thirty different page structures. Worked on 27 of them first try.

The three it missed had pricing behind interactive elements. Still a human problem for now.

Where this gets interesting for GTM:

🕷️ Prospect research that actually reads their website instead of just pulling firmographic data
🕷️ Competitive intel that updates itself
🕷️ Job posting analysis that understands what a role description signals about a company's priorities

The teams building browser-based AI workflows right now are going to have a serious data advantage in six months.
posts.cta: What's your most creative use of browser automation right now?

inspired_by.author: Addy Osmani
inspired_by.summary: Dives into browser-based AI agents and web automation tools, showcasing PWAs with embedded LLMs for developer productivity. Covers performance optimizations and security considerations for client-side AI infra.

remixes.label: browser-as-operating-system
remixes.hook: The browser just became a first-class development environment. Not for writing code. For running autonomous workflows that interact with the web the way you do.
remixes.body:
The browser just became a first-class development environment. Not for writing code. For running autonomous workflows that interact with the web the way you do.

I've been building Playwright-based automations for months. The early use cases were straightforward. Scrape this page. Fill out this form. Extract these data points. Useful, but limited.

The shift happening now is qualitative, not quantitative. Browser automation used to mean scripting exact click sequences. Now it means describing an intent and letting the system figure out the execution path.

"Go to this company's careers page, find all engineering roles posted in the last 30 days, and extract the tech stack requirements." That used to be a 200-line script that broke whenever the page layout changed. Now it's a prompt.

The implications for GTM operations are massive. Competitive intelligence that updates daily without manual research. Lead enrichment that pulls from sources APIs don't cover. Content research that synthesizes information across dozens of pages.

But here's . The real advantage isn't any single automation. It's composability. When browser-native AI can chain together multi-step workflows across different sites, you get capabilities that look like having a research assistant on staff.

Except it runs 24/7, costs almost nothing, and doesn't need context on your business because you built the workflow yourself.
remixes.cta: What workflow would you build first if your browser could execute multi-step tasks autonomously?

remixes.label: data-moat-through-browsers
remixes.hook: APIs give you the same data everyone else has. Browser automation gives you data nobody else is collecting. That's where the moat is.
remixes.body:
APIs give you the same data everyone else has. Browser automation gives you data nobody else is collecting. That's where the moat is.

Every enrichment tool, every intent data provider, every competitive intelligence platform pulls from the same sources. When you subscribe to the same tools as your competitors, you get the same insights. There's no edge in commodity data.

Browser automation changes this equation. You can build custom scrapers that pull from sources no API covers. Job postings, product changelogs, pricing pages, community forums, conference speaker lists, public financial filings. Information that's available but not aggregated.

I've been building these pipelines for my own GTM stack. Here's what a typical one looks like.

Step one: identify the 200 accounts I care about. Step two: build automated workflows that visit their careers pages, product pages, and social channels weekly. Step three: detect changes. New roles posted, features shipped, messaging updated. Step four: score those changes as buying signals.

The result is an intelligence layer that updates continuously and surfaces signals my competitors aren't seeing. Because they're relying on the same third-party data vendors as everyone else.

This is the browser automation opportunity that matters. Not replacing manual clicks. Building proprietary data pipelines from public information that nobody else is bothering to collect and structure.
remixes.cta: What public data source do you think is most underutilized for competitive intelligence?

Browser-native AI is quietly becoming the most powerful data weapon available to small teams. And the big companies are sleeping on it because they've already bought enterprise subscriptions to every data vendor on the market.

The absurd part? This technology is accessible to anyone with basic scripting skills. Playwright is free. The AI layer to make it context-aware is cheap. The data advantage is massive.

But most developers are still building chatbots and writing blog posts about prompt engineering. The boring, unsexy work of browser automation is where the actual competitive edge is forming. And the window where this is an arbitrage opportunity won't stay open forever.

Your browser is about to become your most powerful automation tool. Not your IDE. Not your terminal. Your browser.

I've been running Playwright-based automation for months. Scraping, form filling, data extraction. Standard stuff. But the shift happening now is different.

Browser-native AI isn't just automating clicks. It's understanding context. Reading a page, deciding what matters, extracting structured data without you writing a single CSS selector.

Practical example from this week: I needed to pull pricing data from 30 competitor pages. Old way, I'd write 30 different scrapers because every site structures pricing differently. New way, I pointed an LLM at each page and said "extract the pricing tiers." Same prompt. Thirty different page structures. Worked on 27 of them first try.

The three it missed had pricing behind interactive elements. Still a human problem for now.

Where this gets interesting for GTM:

🕷️ Prospect research that actually reads their website instead of just pulling firmographic data
🕷️ Competitive intel that updates itself
🕷️ Job posting analysis that understands what a role description signals about a company's priorities

The teams building browser-based AI workflows right now are going to have a serious data advantage in six months.

platforms.newsletter:
I've been running browser-based automations for months now and the capability shift happening is worth paying attention to.

It used to be about scripting clicks. Now it's about describing intent and letting AI figure out the execution. The difference is enormous for GTM operations, competitive research, and lead enrichment.

This week I want to show you what a real browser automation pipeline looks like and why I think it's one of the most underrated tools available to solo operators right now.
